subsystem: ICU4C (International Components for Unicode) common library string handling. Specifically the private worker routine UnicodeString::doAppend(const UChar*, int32_t, int32_t), the shared implementation behind UnicodeString::append() and operator+=. It grows a UTF-16 (UChar) string object by copying srcLength code units from a caller-supplied buffer onto the end of the existing string, reallocating the backing array when necessary.
provenance: Identified by the class-qualified method name UnicodeString::doAppend and its UChar/int32_t signature, the ICU-specific helpers u_strlen, us_arrayCopy, cloneArrayIfNeeded, getGrowCapacity, getArrayStart, isBufferWritable, setToBogus, and setLength, and the ICU idioms (isWritable/isBogus, the UChar 16-bit code unit type). The sample banner filename train_0006_cwe190.cpp and the provenance tag 'primevul_validation_0346_vuln' further confirm this is the ICU common/unistr.cpp doAppend implementation. This is the exact function fixed in ICU 67 for CVE-2020-10531.
trust_boundary: doAppend is an internal method but sits at the bottom of every public append path (append(const UChar*, int32_t), append(const UnicodeString&), operator+=, appendString, and the getAppendBuffer/append pattern). Its inputs — srcChars, srcStart, and especially srcLength — flow from application code that may itself be feeding attacker-controlled data: file/document lengths, network payload sizes, decoded/decompressed text lengths, or lengths derived from other overflow-prone arithmetic. The security-critical quantity is srcLength (a 32-bit signed length) combined with the object's current length(); if their sum can be driven near INT32_MAX the append becomes hostile. The existing srcString buffer contents are trusted, but the length metadata governing the copy is the untrusted lever.

what_the_code_does.code: UnicodeString::doAppend(const UChar *srcChars, int32_t srcStart, int32_t srcLength) {
what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the definition of ICU's private append worker. srcChars is a pointer to a buffer of UChar (16-bit UTF-16 code units); srcStart is an offset (in code units) into that buffer from which copying should begin; srcLength is the number of code units to append, with the special convention that a negative value means 'NUL-terminated, measure it yourself'. The method returns *this (a UnicodeString&) so appends can be chained. Because both srcStart and srcLength are int32_t, all length arithmetic in the body is 32-bit signed and can overflow.

The early-out guard. If the target object is not writable (bogus/aliased read-only), or the caller explicitly asked to append zero characters, or the source pointer is NULL, the function does nothing and returns the unchanged string. Note this guard only rejects srcLength == 0 exactly; a negative srcLength is allowed through here (it is the 'auto-measure' sentinel handled below), and a large positive srcLength is also allowed through unchecked.

The source pointer is advanced by srcStart code units so all subsequent logic can treat srcChars as the true start of the region to copy; the comment above tells the reader srcStart must not be used again. There is no validation that srcStart is non-negative or that srcChars+srcStart still points inside the caller's allocation — doAppend trusts its callers to have range-checked srcStart against the source buffer.

Implements the negative-length convention: when the caller passes srcLength < 0, the source is treated as a NUL-terminated UChar string and its length is measured with u_strlen. If the measured length is zero, there is nothing to append and the function returns. After this block srcLength is guaranteed to be a positive int32_t, but it can be as large as u_strlen reports (up to INT32_MAX-1), or as large as the caller passed if it was already positive.

what_the_code_does.code:
int32_t oldLength = length();
  int32_t newLength = oldLength + srcLength;
what_the_code_does.explanation: oldLength captures the current number of code units in the string. newLength is computed as the sum of the current length and the number of code units to append — this is the total size the backing array must hold after the append. This single addition is the crux of the routine's memory safety: newLength is what every downstream capacity decision and the final setLength(newLength) rely on. Both operands are 32-bit signed, so the sum is a 32-bit signed addition with no overflow guard.

The self-append / overlap guard. oldArray is the start of this object's own backing buffer. The two pointer comparisons ([oldArray, oldArray+oldLength) overlapping [srcChars, srcChars+srcLength)) detect the case where the source range overlaps the destination's own storage — e.g. str.append(str). In that case appending in place could reallocate the destination and invalidate srcChars mid-copy, so the code first materializes an independent copy of the source (UnicodeString copy(srcChars, srcLength)), checks that allocation succeeded (isBogus), and then recurses with the copy's storage and srcStart 0. This is a correctness/aliasing safeguard, not a length check.

The reallocation decision. If the already-allocated capacity is large enough to hold newLength AND the buffer is writable in place, the existing array is reused. Otherwise cloneArrayIfNeeded(newLength, getGrowCapacity(newLength)) is called to (re)allocate a buffer big enough for newLength code units (getGrowCapacity computes a growth-padded target). Whichever branch succeeds, the code below assumes the backing array now has room for newLength UChars. This branch is where a corrupted (e.g. negative) newLength silently defeats the growth: a negative newLength satisfies newLength <= getCapacity() and takes the reuse path without allocating anything.

The actual mutation. newArray is the (possibly reallocated) destination buffer. The srcChars != newArray + oldLength test skips the copy in the getAppendBuffer optimization, where the caller already wrote directly into the string's append region so the bytes are already in place. Otherwise us_arrayCopy(srcChars, 0, newArray, oldLength, srcLength) copies srcLength code units from srcChars into newArray starting at index oldLength — i.e. it writes newArray[oldLength .. oldLength+srcLength-1]. Finally setLength(newLength) records the new logical length. The write bound here is oldLength + srcLength code units, exactly the newLength value computed at line 19 and used to size the buffer at line 36.

Returns the (now longer) string to support chaining. If neither the capacity check nor cloneArrayIfNeeded succeeded (allocation failure), the block at 38-49 is skipped and the string is returned unchanged — an out-of-memory path that leaves the object valid.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
int32_t oldLength = length();
  int32_t newLength = oldLength + srcLength;
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Signed 32-bit integer overflow. oldLength and srcLength are both int32_t and both can be large (oldLength up to the current INT32-bounded string length; srcLength up to INT32_MAX-1 from a caller or from u_strlen at line 13). When oldLength + srcLength exceeds INT32_MAX the addition overflows: it is technically undefined behavior, and on real compilers wraps to a negative newLength. There is NO check that newLength >= oldLength (a sum smaller than either operand is the tell-tale of overflow) and no clamp against a maximum string length before newLength is used to size the buffer. This is the root defect (CWE-190) and it directly poisons the capacity logic at line 36 and the copy length at line 47. This is CVE-2020-10531, fixed in ICU 67 by adding an overflow guard that sets the string bogus when the sum overflows.

Heap buffer overflow — the exploitation of the line-19 overflow. When newLength wrapped negative, the test newLength <= getCapacity() is TRUE (a negative value is <= any capacity), so the reuse branch is taken and cloneArrayIfNeeded is never called: the backing array is NOT grown. Yet us_arrayCopy at line 47 still copies the full, un-wrapped srcLength code units starting at oldLength, writing far past the end of the small existing buffer. Because srcLength is enormous (near INT32_MAX) while the buffer holds only oldLength code units, this is a massive out-of-bounds heap write (CWE-787) attacker-influenced through the length that produced the overflow. The invariant the implementation must uphold: reject/bogus the append whenever oldLength + srcLength overflows or exceeds the maximum representable string length, BEFORE using newLength for the capacity check or the copy.

Corrupted length metadata. Even in cases where the copy did not immediately crash, setLength(newLength) records the wrapped/negative newLength as the object's logical length. A negative or nonsensical length stored in the UnicodeString then propagates to every subsequent operation on the object (indexing, further appends, extraction), turning a single overflow into persistent state corruption and additional out-of-bounds reads/writes downstream. The fix must ensure newLength is a validated non-negative in-range value before it is ever stored.

Unvalidated source offset (latent, caller-dependent). srcStart is added to srcChars with no bound check. If a caller passes a srcStart that is negative or larger than the source buffer, srcChars is moved outside the valid region and the subsequent u_strlen (line 13) and us_arrayCopy (line 47) perform out-of-bounds reads (CWE-125). doAppend places the entire burden of validating srcStart on its callers; the public append wrappers are responsible for clamping srcStart into [0, srcBufferLength]. Confirm every caller path does so.

Pointer arithmetic on an attacker-influenced length. srcChars + srcLength is computed for the overlap test using the same large srcLength involved in the overflow. Forming a pointer past the end of the source object is undefined behavior and, with a huge srcLength, the comparison can wrap or mis-evaluate, causing the self-append safeguard to be skipped or wrongly taken. This is a secondary consequence of not bounding srcLength up front; it is not itself the primary memory-safety hole but reinforces that srcLength must be validated before any arithmetic uses it.

summary: This is ICU4C's UnicodeString::doAppend, the core string-append worker. It computes newLength = oldLength + srcLength (line 19) in 32-bit signed arithmetic with no overflow guard; a caller-controlled srcLength near INT32_MAX makes newLength wrap negative, which passes the 'newLength <= getCapacity()' capacity check (line 36) so the buffer is never grown, yet us_arrayCopy (line 47) still copies the full srcLength code units — a remotely-reachable heap buffer overflow. This is CVE-2020-10531 (fixed in ICU 67). Secondary issues: the wrapped length is also stored via setLength, and srcStart (line 9) is added to the source pointer without validation.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: doAppend(const UChar *srcChars, int32_t srcStart, int32_t srcLength) signature
reviewed_anchors.reason: int32_t length parameters flow into unchecked additive arithmetic that sizes a heap copy
attacker-influenced length; drives the overflowing sum and the copy count

reviewed_anchors.anchor: srcStart parameter used at line 9 (srcChars += srcStart)
reviewed_anchors.reason: added to source pointer with no bound check; latent OOB read on hostile caller
input rejection guard; only excludes exact-zero length and NULL, carries no unsafe write itself
resolves negative sentinel to a bounded positive length; the hazard is the later addition, not the measurement
reads existing validated length; safe operand on its own
unguarded signed 32-bit addition — the root integer overflow (CWE-190)
pointer arithmetic srcChars + srcLength on a huge length is UB and can mis-evaluate the safeguard
aliasing safeguard; recursion passes srcStart 0 and the same length, no new contract beyond srcLength itself
a negative wrapped newLength satisfies the check and skips reallocation — the mechanism that turns the overflow into an OOB write
getAppendBuffer copy-skip; correctness optimization, no unsafe write introduced
copies srcLength code units into a buffer sized by the corrupted newLength — the actual out-of-bounds write (CWE-787)
stores the wrapped/negative length as object state, propagating corruption

findings.title: Integer overflow in newLength = oldLength + srcLength leads to heap buffer overflow
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow leading to heap buffer overflow (undersized allocation, oversized copy)
findings.invariant: The total length must be validated before use: reject or set the string bogus when oldLength + srcLength overflows int32_t or exceeds the maximum representable string length (e.g. check newLength < 0 || newLength < oldLength before sizing the buffer).
findings.failure_mode: A caller-influenced srcLength near INT32_MAX (passed directly or measured by u_strlen) makes oldLength + srcLength overflow 32-bit signed arithmetic and wrap to a negative newLength. The negative newLength then satisfies 'newLength <= getCapacity()' at line 36, so cloneArrayIfNeeded is skipped and the backing array is NOT enlarged, while us_arrayCopy at line 47 still copies the full srcLength code units starting at oldLength — writing far past the small existing heap buffer. Result: attacker-influenced out-of-bounds heap write (CWE-787), memory corruption, and potential remote code execution when srcLength derives from untrusted document/network sizes.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no guard rejects an overflowing/oversized newLength between line 19 and the line-36 capacity check; verify the shipped fix (ICU 67) adds a check that sets the string bogus on overflow.

findings.title: Wrapped newLength defeats capacity check, so oversized us_arrayCopy overflows the un-grown buffer
findings.anchor: if((newLength <= getCapacity() && isBufferWritable()) || cloneArrayIfNeeded(...)) { ... us_arrayCopy(srcChars, 0, newArray, oldLength, srcLength);
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds heap write (undersized destination, full-length copy)
findings.invariant: The value used to decide whether to grow the buffer must be the same monotonically-correct total that bounds the copy; a length that is smaller than oldLength must never pass the capacity check, and the copy count (srcLength) must fit within the allocated capacity minus oldLength.
findings.failure_mode: Because newLength was corrupted to a negative value by the line-19 overflow, 'newLength <= getCapacity()' is trivially true and reallocation is skipped, but us_arrayCopy still copies the true (huge) srcLength code units into the small buffer at offset oldLength. This is the concrete out-of-bounds write realizing the CWE-190 root cause. Even absent overflow, this branch assumes newLength correctly reflects srcLength; any mismatch between the sizing value and the copy length is exploitable.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify that whenever the reuse branch is taken, getCapacity() truly accommodates oldLength + srcLength, and that no code path allows the copy length to exceed capacity - oldLength.

findings.title: Corrupted newLength stored via setLength, propagating length corruption to the object
findings.bug_class: State corruption via unvalidated length assignment
findings.invariant: Only a validated, non-negative, in-range length may be committed as the object's logical length.
findings.failure_mode: The wrapped/negative newLength from line 19 is written as the string's length. Subsequent operations (indexing, extract, further appends) then trust a nonsensical length, causing additional out-of-bounds reads/writes and turning a one-shot overflow into persistent object-state corruption. Fixing line 19 also fixes this, but it demonstrates the blast radius of the missing guard.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm setLength rejects or asserts on negative/oversized values, or that newLength is guaranteed valid before reaching this call after the overflow fix.

findings.title: srcStart added to source pointer without bounds validation (latent OOB read)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via unvalidated pointer offset
findings.invariant: Every caller must guarantee 0 <= srcStart <= srcBufferLength and that srcChars+srcStart still lies within the source allocation; doAppend performs no such check.
findings.failure_mode: A negative or too-large srcStart moves srcChars outside the caller's buffer. The later u_strlen (line 13, when srcLength<0) and us_arrayCopy (line 47) then read out of bounds, leaking adjacent heap memory or crashing. Because doAppend is private, the risk depends on the public append wrappers correctly clamping srcStart; this is a latent contract to audit rather than a self-contained bug in the shown code.
findings.what_to_confirm: Audit all public append/appendString/operator+= entry points to confirm srcStart is range-checked against the source buffer length before doAppend is invoked.

findings.title: Pointer arithmetic srcChars + srcLength in the self-append guard is UB for huge lengths
findings.anchor: oldArray < srcChars + srcLength && srcChars < oldArray + oldLength
findings.bug_class: Undefined pointer arithmetic / mis-evaluated overlap check
findings.invariant: srcLength must be validated as a sane, in-range length before it is used to form the pointer srcChars + srcLength; overlap detection must not rely on pointer arithmetic that can overflow.
findings.failure_mode: With srcLength near INT32_MAX, srcChars + srcLength forms a pointer far past the source object (undefined behavior) and the comparison may wrap or mis-evaluate, causing the self-append safeguard to be skipped or wrongly triggered. This is a downstream symptom of the same unbounded srcLength; bounding srcLength up front removes it.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm srcLength is validated before line 19/line 24 so the overlap-check pointer arithmetic stays within a single object.

audit_checklist: Confirm a guard exists between line 19 and the line-36 capacity check that detects oldLength + srcLength overflow (e.g. newLength < 0 || newLength < oldLength) and sets the string bogus / returns without copying — the CVE-2020-10531 fix.

Verify getCapacity()/cloneArrayIfNeeded truly guarantee the destination holds oldLength + srcLength UChars before us_arrayCopy runs, and that a negative newLength can never satisfy 'newLength <= getCapacity()'.
Check that setLength rejects negative or oversized lengths, or that newLength is provably valid at line 49 after the overflow fix.

Audit every public append entry point (append(const UChar*, int32_t), appendString, operator+=, getAppendBuffer users) to confirm srcStart is clamped to [0, srcLength/buffer bound] before doAppend is called.
Confirm ICU's kMaxCapacity / maximum string length cap is enforced so srcLength (including u_strlen results) cannot drive newLength beyond the representable range.
Verify the self-append overlap path materializes an independent copy correctly and that srcLength is bounded before the pointer arithmetic at lines 24-25 is evaluated.
